title: How One Mom's 'Silly' Trivia Business Became a Community Empire
guestBio: Christina Wooldridge is the founder of Trivia Goat, a live entertainment company bringing tech-enabled trivia to bars, breweries, and restaurants. With a background in hotel/restaurant management and nonprofit fundraising, she's a working mom of two based in Orange County, CA who transformed her hospitality expertise into a thriving community-building business.
excerpt: What if your 'silly' business idea could outperform the serious ones? Christina Wooldridge turned a simple trivia concept into a thriving community empire while navigating motherhood and redefining what success really means.
metaDescription: Discover how Christina Wooldridge built Trivia Goat into a community-centered business while being a working mom. Hospitality meets entrepreneurship.
keyTakeaways: Hospitality isn't a soft skill—it's a leadership superpower that creates real power in any conversation.
Your business idea doesn't need to be prestigious to be valuable and profitable.
Human connection, not just products or services, is what keeps customers coming back long-term.
Scaling doesn't always mean doing more—sometimes it means packaging your expertise differently.
Motherhood sharpens leadership skills through forced intentionality and laser focus.
The real value in business comes from creating belonging and community, not just transactions.
Unconventional ideas often succeed because they solve real human needs that others overlook.

pullQuotes.text: Hospitality is not a soft skill — it's a leadership superpower
Your business idea doesn't have to be 'prestigious' to be valuable
The real thing that keeps customers coming back is human connection

timestamps.label: Christina's through line — from childhood parades to live events
Motherhood, identity, and finding value beyond productivity
What Trivia Goat is and how it works

content:
What if the business idea you've been dismissing as "silly" or "not serious enough" could actually become your most successful venture? That's exactly what happened to Christina Wooldridge, founder of Trivia Goat, who turned what seemed like a simple concept into a thriving community-building empire.

In this powerful conversation with Aimee Rickabus on The Manage Her Podcast, Christina shares how she transformed her hospitality background into a tech-enabled trivia business that's creating real connection in an increasingly disconnected world. But this isn't just a business success story—it's a masterclass in redefining value, embracing unconventional ideas, and building something meaningful while navigating the complexities of working motherhood.

The Thread That Connects Everything

Christina's entrepreneurial journey didn't start with a business plan—it started with childhood parades. As a young girl organizing neighborhood festivities, she was unknowingly developing the skills that would later become her superpower: bringing people together and creating experiences that matter. This thread of community-building wove through her hotel and restaurant management career, into nonprofit fundraising, and ultimately into founding Trivia Goat.

What's fascinating about Christina's path is how she recognized this pattern in her own life. Too often, we dismiss our natural inclinations as "not real work" or "just something we're good at." Christina shows us that these innate abilities—especially when they involve connecting people—can become the foundation of a profitable, meaningful business.

Motherhood as Leadership Training

One of the most striking insights from this conversation is how Christina views motherhood as leadership training rather than a career obstacle. When she became a mother, the usual quick fixes and band-aid solutions stopped working. Motherhood forced her to think long-term, to be intentional with her time, and to focus on what truly matters.

This shift in perspective transformed not just her parenting but her entire approach to business. The scattered, people-pleasing version of herself had to evolve into someone who could make clear decisions, set boundaries, and operate with laser focus. As Christina puts it, motherhood didn't diminish her professional capabilities—it sharpened them.

The revelation that her worth wasn't tied to her productivity was game-changing. In a culture that equates busy with important, Christina learned to separate her identity from her output. This mindset shift freed her to pursue ideas that genuinely excited her rather than those that simply looked impressive on paper.

Hospitality as Your Secret Weapon

If you've ever worked in hospitality, you might not realize you're sitting on a goldmine of transferable skills. Christina makes a compelling case that hospitality isn't a "soft skill"—it's a leadership superpower that creates real power in any conversation or situation.

Think about what hospitality really means: reading people's needs before they voice them, making others feel comfortable and valued, managing multiple priorities seamlessly, and turning potentially negative experiences into positive ones. These aren't nice-to-have abilities—they're essential leadership competencies that translate directly into business success.

Christina discovered that her hospitality background gave her a unique advantage in difficult conversations and challenging situations. The ability to create connection and find common ground isn't just useful for managing restaurant customers—it's invaluable for negotiating deals, managing team conflicts, and building lasting business relationships.

The Human Connection Advantage

In an increasingly digital world, Christina identified a crucial gap: genuine human connection. Trivia Goat isn't just about asking questions and keeping score—it's about creating spaces where people feel they belong. The technology enables the experience, but the human element drives the value.

What many venue owners misunderstand, according to Christina, is what actually keeps customers coming back. It's not just good food, competitive prices, or convenient location—though those matter. It's the feeling that they're part of something, that they're known and welcomed, that their presence matters to the community.

This insight extends far beyond the hospitality industry. In whatever business you're building, the companies that thrive long-term are those that prioritize human connection alongside their core offering. People don't just buy products or services—they buy belonging, recognition, and the feeling that they matter.

Scaling Without Sacrificing Presence

One of the biggest challenges for any service-based business is scaling without losing the personal touch that made it special in the first place. Christina solved this through what she calls a "self-serve model"—not in the traditional sense, but by packaging her expertise and systems in a way that maintains quality while allowing growth.

The key was recognizing that she didn't need to be physically present at every trivia night to ensure success. By developing robust training systems, clear processes, and empowering her team members, she created a business that could grow without requiring her constant oversight. This approach allowed her to maintain her presence as a mother while building a scalable enterprise.

This is a crucial lesson for any entrepreneur, especially working mothers: scaling doesn't always mean doing more yourself. Sometimes it means stepping back and creating systems that allow others to deliver your vision consistently.

The Moment Everything Clicked

There's a powerful moment Christina describes when she realized she wasn't just running trivia nights—she was creating belonging. Watching strangers become friends, seeing regular customers who planned their weeks around trivia night, witnessing the genuine community that formed around what started as a "silly" business idea—this is when she understood the real value of her work.

This realization is crucial for any entrepreneur questioning whether their idea is "serious enough" or "important enough." Value isn't determined by complexity or prestige—it's determined by how well you solve real human needs. Christina's business succeeds because it addresses a fundamental human need for connection and community in an accessible, enjoyable way.

Permission to Pursue the Unconventional

Perhaps the most important message from Christina's story is the permission to explore unconventional ideas. In a world that often values traditional markers of success, it takes courage to pursue something that others might dismiss as frivolous or unimportant.

Christina's advice is clear: give yourself permission to explore what genuinely interests you, even if it doesn't fit conventional definitions of a "serious business." The market will tell you whether your idea has value—but only if you're brave enough to test it.

Your unconventional idea might just outperform the serious ones because it solves real problems in ways that others overlook. Sometimes the best businesses come from following your natural inclinations and trusting that what brings you joy might also bring value to others.

Christina Wooldridge's journey from hospitality professional to community-building entrepreneur proves that success doesn't require choosing between meaningful work and profitable business. By embracing her natural abilities, leveraging her unique perspective as a working mother, and having the courage to pursue an unconventional idea, she built something that creates both financial success and genuine human connection.

quiz.title: What's Your Connection Catalyst Style?
quiz.description: Discover how you naturally bring people together and build community. Just like Christina Wooldridge turned her gift for connection into a thriving business empire, you have your own unique way of creating belonging.

questions.question: When you walk into a room full of strangers, what's your natural instinct?

options.text: Look for ways to get conversations started—maybe suggest an icebreaker or find common ground to share
Find one or two people to have deeper conversations with rather than working the whole room
Notice who looks uncomfortable and naturally gravitate toward including them
Observe the room dynamics and think about how the space or setup could better serve everyone

questions.question: You have an idea that excites you, but others might think it's 'not serious enough.' How do you typically respond?

options.text: Get even more excited—if it brings joy, it's probably filling a real need people have
Take time to research and validate it quietly before sharing with anyone else
Start talking to people to understand what problem this might actually be solving for them
Begin sketching out how this could work systematically and what it might look like at scale

questions.question: When people describe what you bring to a team or relationship, they usually mention:

options.text: Your ability to make everything more fun and memorable
Your reliability and the way you really listen to what matters to them
Your knack for knowing exactly what someone needs, often before they realize it themselves
Your talent for seeing the bigger picture and helping everyone understand how their part fits

questions.question: You're planning an event or gathering. What aspect do you focus on first?

options.text: What will make this feel special and different from everything else people attend
Making sure every person who comes will feel comfortable and genuinely welcome
Understanding what people really need from this experience, beyond what they're saying they want
Creating a framework that could work for this event and future ones too

questions.question: Your biggest frustration with how most businesses operate is:

options.text: They're so focused on being 'professional' that they forget to be human and enjoyable
They prioritize quick wins over building real, lasting relationships with people
They solve surface problems instead of paying attention to what customers actually need
They make everything more complicated than it needs to be instead of creating simple, effective systems

questions.question: When you think about your ideal work situation, what energizes you most?

options.text: Creating something that brings people joy and gives them stories to tell later
Building something sustainable that will genuinely serve people for years to come
Solving real problems for people, especially ones that others have overlooked or dismissed
Taking your knowledge and expertise and packaging it so more people can benefit from it

results.title: The Experience Creator
results.description: You're the one who turns ordinary moments into memorable experiences. Like Christina organizing childhood parades, you instinctively know how to make people feel special and included. Your superpower lies in seeing possibilities where others see just another Tuesday. This episode will show you how to recognize the business potential in what might feel like 'just being yourself' and help you understand why your seemingly simple ideas could be your most valuable ones.

results.title: The Steady Cultivator
results.description: You build deep, lasting connections through consistency and genuine care. While others chase the spotlight, you're creating the foundation that everything else stands on. Your approach to community-building is methodical and intentional—qualities that become even sharper under pressure. Christina's insights about motherhood as leadership training will resonate deeply with you, showing how your deliberate, thoughtful approach to relationships is actually a rare and valuable business skill.

results.title: The Bridge Builder
results.description: You have a natural gift for seeing what people need and connecting them with solutions—or with each other. You understand that real value comes from relationships, not just transactions. Your ability to spot gaps and fill them is exactly what makes unconventional businesses thrive. This episode will inspire you to trust your instincts about what people are really craving and show you how hospitality skills can become your secret weapon in any industry.

results.title: The Vision Translator
results.description: You excel at taking big ideas and making them accessible, scalable, and real. You see systems where others see chaos, and you know how to package wisdom in ways people can actually use. Like Christina turning her community-building expertise into a tech-enabled business, you understand that scaling doesn't always mean doing more—sometimes it means doing things differently. This episode will validate your approach to growth and show you how unconventional paths often lead to the most meaningful success.

transcript: 🎶 Music Outro 🎶 Today on The Manager, I'm joined by a woman who understands something many leaders overlook. The experience is leadership, and connection is built on intention. Christina Woldridge is a working mom, entrepreneur, and hospitality-driven experience creator. With a background in hotel and restaurant management and nonprofit development, her work has always centered on service, storytelling, and bringing people together. She's the founder of Trivia Goat, where she leads a team of live hosts creating high-quality high-quality trivia experiences that help venues build real community and repeat business. At the heart of everything Christina does is one core belief. When environments are intentional and welcoming, connection follows. And today, we're going to unpack what that looks like in business, leadership, and life. Christina, welcome to The Manager. So happy to have you. Thank you. I'm super excited. I appreciate the opportunity. Yay. So before we talk about Trivia Go, I wanted to start with you. When you look back over your career from hospitality to nonprofits to entrepreneurship, what through line do you see now that maybe you didn't see at the time? So thank you. I feel like now that I'm in this entrepreneurial side, I've had some time to reflect on how I got here. And so that through line has been pretty apparent in the last couple of years. And it's exciting to find when you can kind of identify it, right? So for me, you know, you touched on it in the intro earlier. hospitality, right? And what that has meant for me is just really service to others in a hospitable way, in a creative way, in a fun way. I was a hotel restaurant major in college. So that's what I majored in and always loved events, but was still trying to find my footing after college of like, do I want to work in a hotel? Do I want to work in a restaurant? Like, what is this going to look like? Was always seeking kind of purpose, purposeful work. And when I realized that I liked the skill set hospitality provided. I loved serving others, creating memories, creating environments. It kind of led me to fundraising, which was not on my radar. It's not something we chatted about in college. It wasn't like a subset of what we were learning, but it had all the same things. It was about relationship cultivation. It was about great events. It was about creating impressions, being of service to others. But for me, it just had an additional purpose to it. So at the end of the night, when I was shutting down an event, it wasn't just that was fun. It was like, oh, we raised, you know, $200,000 for this really great cause and we get to do it again. So that has always kind of been a part of me. And I've kind of taken that skill set just into different avenues, just into different roles. And I like to share the story of, like when I was a child, you know, I played outside a ton and I had a little brother. So he always just had to do what I instructed him to do, right? And creating like plays. The big one was parades for me. Always stuffy parades on the hour, every hour, because I knew somebody out there was watching, you know, I was always upping the game with my parades. But even from childhood, I can see like this idea of, you know, moments of fun moments, you know, environments of creativity were just always who I was and always who I have been. Yeah. That's so fun. It's funny how we can see who we were going to become. Someone told me, just do what you wanted to do when you were four years old and you'll be happy. I think it was Catherine Letson, actually. I forget which episode she is, but it was a fantastic one. And she basically went back to doing what she wanted to do when she was a little kid and She's so happy now. Yeah. And then, you know, it may not have been so specific, like, oh, you know, I want to be a lawyer or a doctor. I think that was kind of the challenging part for me was my road hasn't been specific. It's more of like skills that I like to use and things I like to hone in on. But yeah, you look back in childhood and you're like, well, I was, you know, planning events on the hour, like since I was six. So why wouldn't this be something that leads into adulthood? Right. Totally. It makes sense to me. I see it in my kids and I'm like, oh, you're bound. Indy is bound to boss something. I get it. I see it in her. There's no way that she's not going to be leading, managing in some way. So you're a working mom running a business built around live experiences. How has motherhood shaped the way you lead, create and think about connection? Yeah. So live experiences, you know, you get one shot. And it's just like, you're on, you're on. Motherhood has really, I mean, motherhood's a whirlwind. Let's just put it that way. I mean, it kind of kicked my butt in the beginning because, yeah, I didn't know how to navigate both or do either well. I was trying to do both at hyper speed levels and that wasn't accomplishing very much. So, you know, that in itself has been a journey. But I would say that in translation to how it's impacted my business and the events that we produce, you know, I've become really hyper-focused on just like addressing what needs to be addressed and not getting so involved with all the details, right? You only have a limited amount of time, at least I do, that I can dedicate to work. I try to be as present as possible when my kids are around and the times I've allotted for that. So it's just, you know, it's caused me to just be very intentional about about when I'm working you know these are the problems we're solving and I'm actually finding myself looking for more long-term solutions versus the quick fixes right where in the past it might have been like you know okay whatever let's just cover this whole move forward it's like I don't want to see this problem again like I don't have time to see this problem again so I need to be really intentional about how we find this solution and we need to kind of like package it up and send it off and move on to the next thing so I think it's just being more mindful of my time and how I'm spending that and, you know, being just very focused on the tasks that need to get done and then, you know, spending some time on the big picture and then being able to shut it down for a minute and move on and come back to it. But yeah, it's just a lot of intentionality. It's been kind of necessary to kind of accomplish both. Yeah. It's a different season of life. We were talking about it when the camera was off, you know, how different the season of motherhood is, you know, and what a special time it is too, like honoring ourselves as mothers and making sure we don't miss these like kind of beautiful and special little snowflake moments of motherhood while we're in the pursuit of business. It's great to be in business, but it's like, how do we balance both? A hundred percent. And, you know, I think Most of my life until I became a mother, my value was really centered around my work. And for better or worse, right? So when that's kind of stripped from you, when you producing in the same way isn't what your value is equating to, it's a really hard thing to understand and to connect that motherhood is also, you know, so valuable and provides so much. But you have to be able to see that and really own it. And it took me a while to get there, honestly. Because when you're not producing results and numbers and finances or more accounts or whatever, where people can look at you and be like, oh, wow, you're doing a great job. And it's hard to be like, well, I got my kid to sleep by eight. I mean, that's a feat, right? You know, it's like, nobody cares that I got my kid to sleep at eight. But actually, you know, those things matter. So yeah, it's for me, you know, I'm like, My daughter's five, my oldest. So I'm only five years in. I assume there's still tons to learn, which I'm sure I can connect with you on as you get into preteens and all that good stuff. Oh, it gets wild though. It just, it just gets different, but it gets, it doesn't honestly, as long as you keep your communication like you do with your kids, you just keep your communication going and just never let them push you away. They try to push you away. You squeeze them tighter. Don't ever let them push you away. And there's, just be like okay fine I give up on you because that's that's the point I was talking to my 16 year old about it yesterday and he said yeah some of his friends have gotten his their parents to just kind of give up on them by being by pushing them away and being so difficult and I was like I remember when he tried it with me at 14 one of my friends said do not let him push you away She was like, Amy, if he tries to push you away, you just squeeze him like a boa constrictor. Do not let him push you away. So I didn't. And it worked. It worked. I got to hang on to him. Yeah. No, it's great advice. Yeah. No, I'm pocketing that. That was a good one because there was a moment there with him that I thought, oh, he wanted to go stay, you know, go. We were going to go to Hawaii. And he's like, let me just go stay at my dad's house. And I thought to myself, oh, man, that would be so much easier. And I don't really feel like dealing with him right now. He was being so difficult. And my friend was like, no. She's like, I remember when my daughter did this at this age and I let her go stay with her dad. And it was the worst thing I ever did in my life. She's like, don't do it. I thought at the time I was like, oh, you're being so dramatic. But I think she was 100 percent on point. You can see it now. No, that's that's big for sure. Yeah. Parenting. It's a whole nother thing. And so hospitality is often seen as soft work, but it's actually logistics, emotional intelligence, crisis management and leadership. When did you realize that this was real power? Yeah. So you're absolutely right. It's a lot of, you know, working in the moment, especially, you know, live events were kind of my bread and butter. And so there's down to something's going to go wrong. You know, you can't really pinpoint it. You don't really know to what degree, but, you know, something will go wrong. So pivoting is essential and doing it with a smile on your face and not kind of tainting the user or, you know, guest experience is probably your biggest goal, right? And it is a superpower. I thought it was something anyone could really do, but the more I did it and the more I was around other people who were involved, I was like, oh, like, they're depending on me to like, address all that, you know, because everyone's like, oh, I don't know, you know, they want to step back. So it is a superpower. And I think it's become more relevant, even just in my daily life. Because what I realized is, there's not a lot of people out there who want to serve you. And that can be, you know, for, you know, I'm shopping around for insurance right now. And it's awful, you know, it's not the most fun phone calls I want to make. And it's very clear that they also don't want to talk to you, you know, like you're not like a huge priority either. And so it is a superpower because I recognize that not a lot of people use it. Creating the human connection in any conversation over the phone, in person, you know, it's huge. And it's gotten me really far specifically in you know, in fundraising too, it's like you can sit down with someone and just be transactional and say like, hey, we need this sponsorship. Hey, you know, do you want to give this much? Yes or no? Great. You'll have a table, have fun. We'll see you later. Or you can work on, hey, what is it you're trying to accomplish with your donation? What is it about the org that you really enjoy? What is it that you're looking to change with your financial gift? You know, and you find out more, you create relationships and now you have this more Long-term, robust relationship that, you know, you don't have to return to every year hoping that they come out for your event, right? So this, you know, this hospitable nature can serve in so many different outlets. Can be your business or just trying to get someone on the phone to help you. You know, I've literally stopped conversations when i knew they were going pretty south to say, let's just start over. um I just want to connect with you. Is there something I can do to make this easier for you and your job? Is there something that I can provide that would make this conversation turn so that we both can leave here with what we need? And, and I do that because, and it'll stop people in their tracks, you know, they'll be like, what, what? Yeah. Less and less, right? I feel like less and less, Christina. It's really shocking sometimes. Yeah. Well, as the generations are going on, there's more technology. There's less actual interpersonal interaction happening. You know, you're younger than me, but the generation a little younger than you has even less social interaction. And those experiences, you know, you have one interaction, you know, it ruins your day and ruins their day. It's just like not worth it. sometimes I'll leave experiences. And I think back when I was a server or worked in a coffee shop, and by the way, I've had about 1400 jobs. I've been in every job from like Sears Portrait Studio to like golf cart person to server. I mean, I've, I've been working since I was 14. So I've been in a lot of places and my experience has always been when I'm just nice and of service to others, my day actually goes by faster. It's not even just about them. It's like it would help me just get through the day and I'd be like, oh, already time to clock out, you know, because you're just like occupied with doing things. So I'm always like, if you only knew, like if you just put some more effort, your day would actually be more fun. Like it's even a benefit for you. So that's always really shocking to me. couldn't agree more. When I worked as a kid in the pharmacy, we had a boutique up front, and if I you just could stand there, or you could interact with the customers, and if you talked to the other employees, and you talk to the customers, and you get involved, the day would go so fast. But if no one was there to talk to still like the longest days of my life. yeah You can feel the hours, literally. So, you know, just Just a mental note for everyone. Just stay busy, connect, and, you know, it'll even make your day better and go faster. I love it. I love it. Trivia Goat isn't just about trivia, though. It's community building. What do venue owners misunderstand about what actually keeps people coming back? Yeah, this is a great question, and I love it because it is about community building. So the way we've approached this business is – I really find people who are looking to kind of fill their time with, you know, experiences in hosting and they get to like go out, host a game, meet people, connect, and then go back to their nine to five, you know, Monday through Friday. And those have been some of the best hosts we have. We've had people who have been with us for over 10 years now because it's just a part of their week. It's just what they look forward to. Because of that, they've built really strong relationships with the players. So teams will come back week after week. They'll bring their other friends. They might build teams from there. But what keeps them coming back is that relationship with our hosts, right? But really it's the partnership that happens at the games. It's just like a very, very strong partnership that people want to walk into. Right. But when it's too siloed, when it's like, oh, that's just the company we hire and we're over here. We try to keep it very, a good variety. So we're not trying to find the smartest person in the room. we're trying to find the most well-rounded, balanced team. So there's pop culture, there's history, there's science, there's 50-50. If you've gotten no questions right and you just need to pick one, there's options there. We have Name That Tune. So we really just try to build a more well-rounded game. We've been to other games where the questions can be really hard. You get to a point where you're like, I'm just opting out because I have no idea. We don't want anyone to leave feeling like they didn't contribute. So that's really our goal. It sounds like fun. Yeah, it's fun. And we do themed games throughout the year. You know, we have a lot of Harry Potter requests and a lot of Harry Potter. Taylor Swift has been a really big one this year. So if you think about question answer, we really can customize any game for any topic, including personal topics. You know, we've done birthday parties or retirement parties where it's like, about the person and stuff. So it's really fun. Like engagement parties. That'd be so fun. Yeah. Like the, uh, the dinner, you know, like the, um, what does it call it? Just the rehearsal dinner. Yes. Yeah. So that's kind of, kind of cool. Never. I love it. I love it so much. So you lead a team of live hosts, right? Um, so what's the hardest part of scaling something that relies so heavily on people, presence and personality? Yeah, I mean, that's a great question. And I would say I'm not even sure, you know, I've gotten the whole picture of what that would look like. So managing people for live events is really hard. It's difficult to find the right person. And then once you find the right person who would make a great host, you also have to find the right location.
